items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

items.q: Where do object pronouns go in 'I see him' (lo veo)?
items.why: Students apply English word order. Spanish places object pronouns BEFORE conjugated verbs. They can attach to infinitives, gerunds, and positive commands - but not after a conjugated verb in normal sentences.

items.q: Where do most descriptive adjectives go relative to the noun?
items.misconception: Before the noun, as in English.
items.random_wrong: Either position with the same meaning.
items.why: Students apply English order. In Spanish, descriptive adjectives typically FOLLOW the noun. Some adjectives precede (limiting adjectives like buen, gran) and can change meaning - gran amigo (great friend) vs amigo grande (big friend).

items.q: When was Part I of Don Quijote published?
items.why: Correct answer: 1605. “1492” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: era. “fue” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Cervantes die?
items.why: Exact answer: 1616. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
